elements.sent-2411-001: Linearity testing has been a focal problem in property testing of functions.
elements.sent-2411-002: We combine different known techniques and observations about linearity testing in order to resolve two recent versions of this task.
elements.sent-2411-003: First, we focus on the online manipulations model introduced by Kalemaj, Raskhodnikova and Varma (ITCS 2022 \& Theory of Computing 2023).
elements.sent-2411-004: In this model, up to $t$ data entries are adversarially manipulated after each query is answered.
elements.sent-2411-005: Ben-Eliezer, Kelman, Meir, and Raskhodnikova (ITCS 2024) showed an asymptotically optimal linearity tester that is resilient to $t$ manipulations per query, but their approach fails if $t$ is too large.
elements.sent-2411-006: We extend this result, showing an optimal tester for almost any possible value of $t$.
elements.sent-2411-007: First, we simplify their result when $t$ is small, and for larger values of $t$ we instead use sample-based testers, as defined by Goldreich and Ron (ACM Transactions on Computation Theory 2016).
elements.sent-2411-008: A key observation is that sample-based testing is resilient to online manipulations, but still achieves optimal query complexity for linearity when $t$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-009: We complement our result by showing that when $t$ is very large, any reasonable property, and in particular linearity, cannot be tested at all.
elements.sent-2411-010: Second, we consider linearity over the reals with proximity parameter $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Fleming and Yoshida (ITCS 2020) gave a tester using $O(1/\varepsilon\ \cdot log(1/\varepsilon))$ queries.
elements.sent-2411-012: We simplify their algorithms and modify the analysis accordingly, showing an optimal tester that only uses $O(1/\varepsilon)$ queries.
elements.sent-2411-013: This modification works for the low-degree testers presented in Arora, Bhattacharyya, Fleming, Kelman, and Yoshida (SODA 2023) as well, resulting in optimal testers for degree-$d$ polynomials, for any constant degree $d$.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the field of property testing\cite{RS96, GGR98 , a randomized algorithm is given oracle access to a large object, and a promise that the object either has some property (YES instances), or is ``far'' from having it (NO instances), under some notion of distance.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The goal of the algorithm is to distinguish between the two cases with success probability at least $2/3$, where the complexity is measured by the number of oracle calls made\footnote{This is well justified, as the running time is typically polynomial in the query complexity.. .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this manuscript, we focus on testing functions over a field $\F$ (in particular $\F = \F_2$ and $\F = \R$).
elements.sent-2411-017: That is, the tester is allowed oracle access to some function $f: \F^n \to \F$ and distinguishes functions with a desired property from ones that are far from any function satisfying the property.
elements.sent-2411-018: The most studied property of functions is linearity, for which a tester was first given in~\cite{BLR93 , perhaps the earliest instance of a property tester in the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The test, dubbed ``the $3$ points test'' simply draws two random points $x,y \in \F^n$ and accepts only if $f(x) + f(y) = f(x \oplus y)$, where $+$ denotes addition over $\F$, and $\oplus$ denotes point-wise addition.
elements.sent-2411-020: By definition, if $f$ is linear, this equality must hold for any pair $x,y$.
elements.sent-2411-021: It was shown in various settings that if $f$ is $\eps$-far from linear, there is an inequality for at least an $\Omega(\eps)$-fraction of the pairs $x,y$.\cite{BLR93,BellareCHKS96,FlemingYoshida20 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Thus, repeating the process $\theta(1/\eps)$ times defines a tester for linearity with the same query complexity.
elements.sent-2411-023: We note this tester satisfies the additional requirement of never rejecting a linear function, making it a one-sided error tester for linearity.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is well known that $\Omega(1/\eps)$ oracle calls are necessary to test linearity (and most reasonable properties~\cite{fischer2024basic ), proving the 3 points test of \cite{BLR93 is optimal in terms of query complexity.}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Online manipulations over the Boolean field
elements.sent-2411-025: Linearity was studied extensively over the Boolean field $\F_2$~\cite{BLR93, BellareS94, FeigeGLSS96, BellareCHKS96, BellareGS98, Trevisan98, SudanT98, SamorodnitskyT00, HastadW03,Ben-SassonSVW03, Samorodnitsky07, SamorodnitskyT09, ShpilkaW06, KaufmanLX10, KalemajRV22 , and an entire survey was dedicated to this problem~\cite{RasR16 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Before we present the online manipulation model, we first review prominent ideas from previous works that we later use.
elements.sent-2411-027: Recently, in the context of online manipulations over $\F = \F_2$ (which we describe soon), the $k$-points test was proposed by~\cite{KalemajRV22 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this test, which generalizes the $3$-point test of \cite{BLR93 , the algorithm chooses points $x_1,\dots, x_{k-1}$ at random, and accepts only if\footnote{We use $[k]$ for the set $\set{1,\dots,k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: It was later shown by~\cite{ben2024property that repeating the $k$-points test $\Theta(1/(k\eps))$ times produces an optimal linearity tester for any $k = O(1/\eps)$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Linearity over $\F_2$ was also considered in~\cite{goldreich2016sample using a weaker oracle access, where the tester cannot retrieve $f(x)$ for a value $x$ of its choice, but instead each oracle call outputs a pair $(x, f(x))$ with a uniformly random choice of $x \in_R \F_2^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: A tester using such oracle access is called a sample-based tester~\cite{GGR98 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: It was shown in~\cite{goldreich2016sample that $\Theta\left(n + 1/\eps\right)$ samples are sufficient and necessary to test linearity.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The gist is that a linear function is determined by its value on a basis $B$ of the entire space $\F_2^n$.
elements.sent-2411-034: After $\Theta(n)$ samples, one can extrapolate some linear function $g$, and use the remaining $1/\eps$ samples to compare $f$ and $g$.
elements.sent-2411-035: A formal argument for this setting, without online manipulations, appears in \autoref{sec:sample_based .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The online manipulation model was recently introduced by~\cite{KalemajRV22 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this model, after each query is answered, $t$ data points go through manipulations.
elements.sent-2411-038: These can be either (1) erasures, where an entry $f(x)$ is replaced with $\bot$; or (2) corruptions, where an entry $f(x)$ can be changed to any value in the range of $f$.
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, they study linearity testing over $\F_2$.
elements.sent-2411-040: First, they note the $3$-points-test is fragile in the presence of an adversary: a tester that insists on querying a triplet of the form $(x, y, x+y)$ must make $\Omega\left( t \right)$ queries, since fewer queries allow the adversary to erase all (new) pairs that were created at each step.
elements.sent-2411-041: To overcome this, they devise the $k$-points test, which creates more combinations, and eventually leads to a tester with query complexity $O(\log t / \eps)$.
elements.sent-2411-042: It was then pointed out in~\cite{ben2024property that the $k$-point test not only preserves the probability of spotting a violation, but actually increases it by a factor of $\approx k$ --- this is enough to reach a query complexity of $O\left(\log t + 1/\eps\right)$, which is optimal.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Indeed, a lower bound of $\Omega(\log t)$ was shown for this model by\cite{KalemajRV22 , and an $\Omega(1/\eps)$ holds even without manipulations.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Another recent work by Minzer and Zheng~\cite{MinzerZ24 showed a striking $O(\log ^{\Theta(d) (t))$ tester for degree-$d$ polynomials over any finite field.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For the special case of linearity ($d=1$) over $\F_2$, however, their result is suboptimal , achieving a query complexity of $O\left(\log^6(t/\eps)/\eps\right)$.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: How many manipulations can the tester handle?
elements.sent-2411-046: All the above testers share a similar limitation: they all require $t \leq 2^{n/c}$ for some $c \geq 2$ (See~\autoref{table:online_ersures_results for the precise values).}
elements.sent-2411-047: It is natural to ask what is the highest value of $t$ for which linearity is still testable.
elements.sent-2411-048: Clearly, $t = 2^n$ is too much, but how close can we get? In \autoref{sec:online_manipulations we fully resolve this question.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Our observation, roughly speaking, is that all previous testers include some queries that are ``too predictable'', making them susceptible to adversarial manipulations.
elements.sent-2411-050: However, this only problematic when the adversary has very large manipulation budget $t = 2^{\Omega(n)}$, in which case we can simply apply the sample-based tester instead, killing two birds with one stone.
elements.sent-2411-051: First, sample-based testers almost automatically overcome online manipulations.
elements.sent-2411-052: Second, the seemingly high sample complexity of the tester is actually optimal as in this regime we have $\Theta(\log(t) + 1/\eps) = \Theta(n + 1/\eps)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, in the case of erasure adversary, the tester has one-sided error.
elements.sent-2411-055: While our tester achieves optimal query complexity, it also handles essentially the largest manipulation budget $t$ for which linearity is still testable, making it doubly optimal.
elements.sent-2411-056: Indeed, in~\cref{sec:impossibility_result , we show an impossibility result for the online model with the following implication for linearity:.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The argument we show in~\cref{sec:impossibility_result is actually much more general.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We consider properties of functions $\F_q^n \to \F_q$, where the input size is $N := q^n$, and show that many properties become untestable in the online manipulation model when $t$ is too large.
elements.sent-2411-059: Trivially, $t = N$ makes testing impossible as the entire input can be manipulated after just a single query is answered authentically.
elements.sent-2411-060: We show that many properties are untestable even when $t = \Theta(\eps^2 N)$, which is the strongest generic statement one can hope for (as it is tight with~\cref{thm:online_erasures ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: The intuition is this: consider a property that requires $\Omega(1/\eps)$, and recall $t$ is an $\eps^2$-fraction of the input size.
elements.sent-2411-062: On the one hand, after $c/\eps$ queries (for small enough $c$) the tester still cannot distinguish certain YES and NO instances with high enough probability.
elements.sent-2411-063: On the other hand, at this point an online adversary can already manipulate an $\eps$-fraction of the original input, enough to completely blur the initial difference between a YES and a NO instance.
elements.sent-2411-064: Hence, any additional query is useless, and the tester is doomed to fail.
elements.sent-2411-065: To formally show this for a wide variety of properties, we leverage a recent elegant argument of~\cite{fischer2024basic , that constructs a concrete hard distribution for any property $\mathcal{P}$ that satisfies certain ``sanity'' conditions (to be specified later).}
elements.sent-2411-066: Although our result is stated for functions $f:\F_q^n \to \F_q$, it can be easily adapted to different input types as well.

elements.sent-2411-067: We start by describing the model of testing functions over the reals.
elements.sent-2411-068: It is similar to the case of finite domains, with the important distinction that a uniform measure over the domain no longer exists.
elements.sent-2411-069: This is indeed an issue, as the uniform measure over finite domains is crucial to most standard notions of distance (e.g., hamming distance).
elements.sent-2411-070: Instead, it is natural to choose the standard (multivariate) Gaussian, denoted by $\cN(\bm 0,I)$.
elements.sent-2411-071: More generally, the distribution-free testing framework \cite{GGR98,halevyK07 , considers a distance measure with respect to an unknown but samplable distribution over the domain.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Formally, for a measurable function $f:\R^n\to\R$, a proximity parameter $\eps>0$, an unknown but samplable distribution $\cD$ supported over $\R^n$, and a property $\cP$, $f$ is $\eps$-far from $\cP$ with respect to $\cD$ if\footnote{We use bold face, e.g. $\bm x$, to denote vectors/points in $\R^n$.. \begin{equation}\delta_{\cD}(f,\cP) := \inf_{g\in\cP}\left\{ \Pr_{\bm x\sim\cD}[ f(\bm x) \neq g(\bm x)] \right\}>\eps.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-073: An algorithm is a distribution-free tester for $\cP$, if given query access to $f$, sample access to $\cD$, and $\eps>0$, it accepts all $f\in\cP$ with probability at least $2/3$, and rejects all $f$ such that $\delta_{\cD (f,\cP)>\eps$, with probability at least $2/3$.}

elements.sent-2411-075: While the two are equivalent over finite fields, some pathological examples show that functions over the reals can be additive but not linear \cite{Ham05 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In fact, over finite domains this equivalence is key for testing results, as the $3$-point BLR test directly checks for additivity.

elements.sent-2411-079: In order to describe previous and new testing results over the reals, we first review another useful concept from the testing literature.
elements.sent-2411-080: The notion of self-correction dates back to~\cite{BLR93 and has deep ties with many testing results.}
elements.sent-2411-081: To demonstrate this viewpoint, we revisit the $3$-point linearity tester over $\F_2$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Define the local correction of $p$ according to $x$ as $g_x(p) := f(p+x) - f(x)$, That is, by querying $x$ and $p+x$ we get an indication what the value of $p$ ``should be'' in order to satisfy linearity.
elements.sent-2411-083: Define the global correction to be \begin{equation}g(p) := \maj_{x} g_x(p) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: Think of $g$ as a function that ``corrects'' each $p$ by assigning it the value that satisfies linearity for more choices of $x$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Formally the $3$-point tester asks whether $f(p) = g_x(p)$, but morally it asks whether $f \equiv g$, by comparing them on a random input $p$ and replacing $g$ with a ``representative'' $g_x$ which can be computed by $2$ queries to $f$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Denoting the rejection probability of the test by $\mu$, the tester is analyzed by showing two claims: (1) if $\mu$ is small then $g$ and $f$ are close; and (2) if $\mu$ is small then $g\in \cP$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Combining the claims, a small rejection probability implies that $f$ is close to linear (specifically to $g$), which guarantees the soundness of the tester (by counter positive).
elements.sent-2411-088: To show (1) and (2), a crucial condition is that evaluation of $g_x(p)$ uses queries to $f$ that have the same marginal distribution (here $x$ and $p+x$ are both marginally uniform over the domain).

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{$\blr{k}$ } \label{alg:krv-linearity-test} { \Given {Even integer parameter $k\geq 2$ and query access to a function $f:\{0,1\}^n\to\{-1, 1\}$ } Query $k$ points $x_1,\dots, x_k \in \cube{n}$ chosen uniformly at random (with replacement). Query point $y = \bxor_{i\in[k]} x_i$. {\bf Accept} if $f(y) = \prod_{i\in[k]} f(x_i)$ (equivalently, if $f(y) \cdot \prod_{i\in[k]} f(x_i) = 1$); otherwise, {\bf reject}. }

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Online-Erasure-Resilient Linearity Tester}\label{alg:linearity-main} { \Given {Parameters $\eps\in(0,1/2],t\in\N$; query access to $f$ via $t$-erasure oracle sequence $\oracle{}$ } \RepTimes{$6$} {\label{step:linearity-repeat} Sample $X =(x_1,\dots,x_{m})\in(\cube{n})^m$ uniformly at random. Query $f$ at points $x_1,\dots, x_m$. Query $f$ at point $y = \xor_{j\in S} x_j$, where $S$ is a uniformly random subset of $[m]$ of size $\frac m2$. \If{$\oracle{}(y) \cdot \Pi_{j\in S} \oracle{}\left(x_j\right) = -1$}{Reject \Comment{This implies no erasures in this iteration.} } } Accept }
